game.title: Code Blue: Postpartum & Newborn Care Escape Room
game.subtitle: Associate Degree Nursing Program

instructions.heading: Mission Briefing
paragraphs: You have been assigned to three critical patients. Each case will test your clinical judgment and decision-making skills.
Work through each case one question at a time. The patient chart will update as new information becomes available — just as it would in a real clinical setting.
Answer each question correctly to receive a piece of the unlock code. Once all questions in a room are answered, enter the complete code to advance to the next case.
If you need a hint, one is available for each question — but each hint adds 2 minutes to your total time. Use them wisely.
Your total time is tracked from the moment you begin. When all three rooms are complete, your final escape time will be recorded.
rules: Read the patient chart carefully before answering each question.
The chart grows with each question — new information may change your priorities.
Hints are available but costly — each hint adds 2 minutes to your timer.
Enter the 4-digit room code exactly as shown to unlock the next room.
Work as a team. Good luck, nurses.

chartSections.title: Past Medical History
items: Hypertension

chartSections.title: Prenatal History
items: BMI 31.4 at delivery
GBS negative
Pregnancy complicated by mild anemia (Hgb 10.2 g/dL at 36 weeks)
Uncomplicated prenatal course otherwise

chartSections.title: Delivery Summary — Documented 0230
rows: Vacuum-assisted due to prolonged second stage
Midline episiotomy; repaired with 3-0 absorbable suture
Oxytocin 10 units IM immediately after placenta delivery

chartSections.title: Postpartum Assessment — 0345
rows: Soft, displaced to right, 1 cm above umbilicus
Rubra, heavy, 2 clots noted (approx. 2 cm each)
Patient states she does not feel the urge to void
Alert and oriented x4; reports feeling 'dizzy'

chartSections.title: Provider Orders — 0410
items: Oxytocin 20 units in 1,000 mL LR, infuse at 200 mL/hr
Methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM x1
Misoprostol 800 mcg PR x1
Foley catheter to gravity drainage
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 minutes
Type and crossmatch — 2 units packed red blood cells
CBC, coagulation panel, fibrinogen STAT
Notify provider immediately with any change in status

chartSections.title: Nursing Note — 0412
chartSections.content: Methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM ordered. Medication held. Provider notified. Patient reports history of hypertension treated with medication prior to this pregnancy; prenatal records being retrieved. See physician acknowledgment note.

chartSections.title: Postpartum Assessment — 0430
rows: Firm, midline, at umbilicus after fundal massage and catheterization
300 mL clear yellow urine obtained immediately upon insertion
Alert, anxious, reports feeling 'like something is wrong'
LR with oxytocin infusing; second PIV 16g placed right forearm

questions.stem: Based on the 0345 assessment, which immediate nursing action is most appropriate?

choices.text: Apply an ice pack to the perineum.
Encourage oral fluids and reassess in one hour.
Assist the patient to empty her bladder.
Notify the provider.

questions.hint: Look at the position and quality of the uterine fundus in the 0345 assessment. Consider what anatomical structure, when full, can prevent the uterus from contracting properly.
questions.feedback: Correct. A soft, right-displaced fundus with heavy lochia and clots indicates uterine atony from bladder distension. The immediate nursing actions are fundal massage and bladder emptying to restore uterine tone. Notifying the provider is appropriate if these measures fail.

questions.stem: After having Ms. Delgado empty her bladder and providing fundal massage, the nurse notices that the fundus does not stay firm without massage and lochia has increased. Which findings place Ms. Delgado at highest risk for postpartum hemorrhage? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Second stage of labor duration — 2 hours and 42 minutes
Administration of oxytocin immediately after delivery of placenta
Vacuum-assisted birth
Hemoglobin of 10.2 g/dL at 36 weeks gestation
Obstetric history G3P3
EBL 350 mL

questions.hint: Focus on factors that affect the uterus's ability to contract after delivery. Think about what prolonged or difficult labor does to uterine muscle, and what obstetric history tells you about uterine tone.
questions.feedback: Correct: A, C, E. A prolonged second stage, vacuum-assisted (operative vaginal) delivery, and multiparity (G3P3) are well-established risk factors for uterine atony due to muscle fatigue. Oxytocin administration is a protective intervention. Mild prenatal anemia doesn't cause hemorrhage, though it worsens tolerance to blood loss. An EBL of 350 mL is within expected limits for vaginal delivery.

questions.stem: The provider gives telephone orders. For each order, select whether the nurse should Implement or Hold and Notify the Provider.

choices.text: Oxytocin 20 units in 1,000 mL LR at 200 mL/hr
Methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM x1
Misoprostol 800 mcg PR x1
Tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 minutes
Foley catheter to gravity

questions.hint: Review the patient's Past Medical History carefully. One of these medications has a serious contraindication related to a pre-existing condition documented in this patient's chart.
questions.feedback: Methylergonovine is contraindicated in patients with hypertension — its vasoconstrictive properties can precipitate a hypertensive crisis. The patient's documented history of hypertension makes this medication unsafe. All other orders are appropriate first-line interventions for uterine atony.

questions.stem:
The nurse has assisted Ms. Delgado to empty her bladder and performed fundal massage. Despite these interventions, the fundus does not remain firm and lochia has increased. The provider enters orders at 0410.

Place the nursing actions in the order they should be completed, from first (1) to last (5).

choices.text: Initiate oxytocin 20 units in 1,000 mL LR at 200 mL/hr
Administer misoprostol 800 mcg per rectum
Infuse tranexamic acid 1 g IV over 10 minutes
Draw ordered labs
Insert Foley catheter to gravity drainage

questions.hint: Prioritize actions that directly stop the bleeding first. Then think about what prevents re-distension and supports uterine contraction. Blood preparation and labs are essential but follow immediate hemorrhage-control measures.
questions.feedback: Correct. The first three steps are fixed: 1) Oxytocin (first-line uterotonic), 2) Misoprostol (second uterotonic), 3) Tranexamic acid (reduces ongoing blood loss). Steps 4 and 5 — Draw ordered labs and Insert Foley catheter — may be completed in either order. The Foley prevents bladder re-distension and allows accurate output monitoring; labs provide essential data for ongoing hemorrhage management. Both are accepted sequences.

questions.stem:
The nurse prepares to administer tranexamic acid 1 g IV. The pharmacy supplies tranexamic acid 1,000 mg in a 100 mL bag. The order states to infuse over 10 minutes.

The nurse will administer the medication at _______ mL/hr.
questions.hint: This requires a rate calculation. You have 100 mL to infuse over 10 minutes. Convert minutes to hours to find the hourly rate.
questions.feedback: 600 mL/hr. Calculation: 100 mL ÷ 10 min × 60 min/hr = 600 mL/hr. The entire 100 mL bag is used (1,000 mg = 1 g), and the rate is set to deliver it over 10 minutes.

questions.stem: At 0430, the nurse reassesses Ms. Delgado after interventions. Which finding indicates the interventions have been most effective?

choices.text: Blood pressure 98/60 mmHg
Heart rate 118 bpm
Uterine fundus firm and midline at the umbilicus
Foley output of 300 mL clear urine

questions.hint: The goal of the interventions was to control uterine atony — the source of the hemorrhage. Which finding directly reflects whether that goal was achieved?
questions.feedback: Correct. A firm, midline fundus indicates uterotonic interventions are controlling atony. The BP and HR remain abnormal — the patient is not yet hemodynamically stable. The Foley output explains a precipitating factor (bladder distension) but does not indicate hemorrhage control.

chartSections.title: Newborn Demographics
rows: Breastfeeding, initiated at 30 minutes of life

chartSections.title: Maternal Prenatal History (Relevant)
items: Rh-negative; Rh immunoglobulin administered at 28 weeks gestation
Rh immunoglobulin NOT administered postpartum to mother at time of delivery (documented omission; incident report filed)
GBS negative
No maternal infections

chartSections.title: Newborn Assessment — DOL 1, 0200 (12 hours of life)
rows: Pink with mild yellow tinge noted at face and upper chest
Breastfeeding 8–10 min each side; mother reports infant difficult to latch, sleepy at breast

chartSections.title: Bhutani Nomogram — Reference
chartSections.caption: Hour-specific bilirubin risk designation for newborns ≥35 weeks gestational age. Source: Stanford Medicine Newborn Nursery.

chartSections.title: Provider Orders — DOL 1, 0300
items: Transcutaneous bilirubin (TcB) checks every 4 hours
Total serum bilirubin (TSB) to be drawn if TcB exceeds threshold per nomogram
Lactation consultation ordered
Encourage breastfeeding every 2–3 hours, minimum 8 times per 24 hours
Pediatric provider to reassess at 0600

chartSections.title: Newborn Assessment — DOL 2, 0600 (34 hours of life)
rows: Yellow discoloration extending to abdomen and upper thighs
Breastfed x3 in past 12 hours; mother states infant 'won't wake up to eat'

chartSections.title: Provider Orders — DOL 2, 0630
items: Intensive phototherapy — initiate immediately, double phototherapy
Total serum bilirubin every 4–6 hours
IV access: 24g PIV, scalp or hand
IV fluid: D10W at 80 mL/kg/day
Continue breastfeeding every 2–3 hours; supplement with expressed breast milk or formula 10 mL after each feeding
NPO if bilirubin continues to rise — exchange transfusion team on standby
Neonatology consult — STAT
Serum electrolytes, blood glucose, blood type and crossmatch

questions.stem: The nurse reviews Baby Boy Chen's chart at 12 hours of life. Which findings place this newborn at highest risk for developing significant hyperbilirubinemia? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Late preterm gestational age (37 weeks)
Apgar score of 8 at 1 minute
ABO incompatibility (mother O negative, infant A positive)
Birth weight 2,940 g
Breastfeeding with poor latch and sleepy infant

questions.hint: Consider which factors affect either the production of bilirubin (think about red blood cell destruction) or the ability to clear it (think about liver maturity and feeding adequacy).
questions.feedback: Correct: A, C, E. Late preterm gestational age (immature hepatic conjugation), ABO incompatibility (immune-mediated hemolysis), and poor breastfeeding with sleepy infant (increased enterohepatic circulation) are all established risk factors. Apgar score and birth weight in these ranges are not independent risk factors.

questions.stem: The nurse assesses Baby Boy Chen at 34 hours of life and documents the findings noted in the chart. Which single finding requires the most immediate notification of the provider?

choices.text: Weight loss of 5.4% from birth weight
Anterior fontanelle slightly bulging with high-pitched cry
1 stool in the past 12 hours, transitional
Infant breastfed only 3 times in the past 12 hours

questions.hint: Review the neurological findings in the assessment. Some findings indicate that bilirubin has already begun affecting the brain — these are always the highest priority.
questions.feedback: Correct. A bulging fontanelle combined with a high-pitched cry are neurological signs consistent with the acute phase of bilirubin encephalopathy (acute kernicterus) — a neurological emergency. Weight loss of 5.4% is below the 7–10% threshold for intervention. Decreased stooling is concerning but not immediately urgent when neurological signs are present.

questions.stem: The nurse receives the 0630 provider orders. Place the following nursing actions in the order they should be performed, from first (1) to last (4).

choices.text: Obtain IV access and begin D10W infusion
Contact lactation consultant to support feeding plan
Draw serum electrolytes, blood glucose, and type and crossmatch
Place infant in warmer with eyes shielded for phototherapy

questions.hint: The most urgent order targets the bilirubin directly. Then think about what supports excretion of bilirubin. Labs need to be drawn before the infant is too distressed. The consultant is important but not immediately urgent.
questions.feedback: Correct order: 1) Place infant in warmer with eyes shielded (initiates phototherapy — most urgent intervention), 2) Obtain IV access and begin D10W (hydration supports bilirubin excretion), 3) Draw labs (monitoring and preparation for possible exchange transfusion), 4) Contact lactation consultant (important but not immediately urgent).

questions.stem:
The provider orders D10W IV fluid at 80 mL/kg/day for Baby Boy Chen. His current weight is 2,780 g.

Calculate the infusion rate in mL/hr. Round to the nearest tenth.

Answer: _______ mL/hr
questions.hint: Step 1: Convert grams to kilograms. Step 2: Multiply by the ordered mL/kg/day. Step 3: Divide by 24 hours to get the hourly rate.

questions.stem: At 1000 on DOL 2, the nurse reassesses Baby Boy Chen after double phototherapy has been in place for 3 hours. Which findings indicate the infant's condition is worsening despite current interventions? Select all that apply.

choices.text: TSB increased to 24.1 mg/dL
Opisthotonus noted
High-pitched inconsolable cry
Upward gaze deviation
Infant currently NPO
Bulging anterior fontanelle

questions.hint: Distinguish between findings that represent a worsening clinical response versus planned interventions. Ask yourself: which findings were planned by the care team, and which are new clinical changes?
questions.feedback: Correct: A, B, C, D, F. Rising TSB despite phototherapy, opisthotonus (hyperextension of trunk/neck), high-pitched cry, upward gaze deviation (sunset sign), and bulging fontanelle are all signs of advancing bilirubin encephalopathy. NPO status is a planned intervention in preparation for possible exchange transfusion — it is not a sign of deterioration.

chartSections.title: Patient Demographics
rows: Primary cesarean section (Category II FHR tracing with failure to progress)
Live born female, currently in room with mother
Uncomplicated; discharged home on PPD 2, returned to ED today with husband

chartSections.title: Psychiatric and Social History
items: No prior psychiatric diagnosis
No personal or family history of bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, or postpartum psychosis documented at prenatal visits
Denies current substance use
Married; husband present and actively involved
Adequate housing and social support reported

chartSections.title: Emergency Department Triage — Postpartum Day 5, 1430
chartSections.chiefComplaint: "She hasn't slept in three days, she's saying things that don't make sense, and this morning she told me the baby wasn't hers." — per husband

chartSections.title: Triage Nursing Note — 1435
chartSections.content: Patient arrived ambulatory, accompanied by husband and 5-day-old infant. Patient appears disheveled. Makes intermittent eye contact. Speech is rapid and tangential. Patient states, "I know what they want to do with her. I have to protect her. The nurses at the hospital — they switched my baby. I can see it in her eyes." Husband states patient has not slept since postpartum day 2. Patient intermittently attempts to leave the triage area. Infant appears well-nourished, calm. Infant placed with husband at this time per patient's request.

chartSections.title: ED Provider Assessment — 1510
rows: Patient endorses hearing a voice telling her to 'keep the baby safe from them'
Fixed belief that infant was switched at the hospital; staff planning to take the infant
Elevated, expansive, rapidly shifting to fearful
Absent — patient does not believe she is ill
Denies; states 'I would never hurt her, I have to protect her'
Husband reports approximately 3–4 hours total over past 72 hours

chartSections.title: Laboratory Results — 1520
rows: No WBC, no bacteria, no nitrites

chartSections.title: Provider Orders — 1545
items: Psychiatry consult — STAT
1:1 nursing observation — continuous
Infant to remain with husband or designated support person at all times when mother is acutely symptomatic
Haloperidol 5 mg IM x1, may repeat x1 in 30 minutes if no response
Lorazepam 1 mg IM x1 — available if needed for acute agitation
Patient to be evaluated for voluntary vs. involuntary psychiatric admission
Social work consult
Lactation consult — consider breastfeeding safety in context of medications ordered

chartSections.title: Nursing Assessment — 1600
rows: Patient pacing in room, repeatedly asking to leave, states she needs to 'get the baby away from here'
Medication administered at 1550; no response noted yet

chartSections.title: Nursing Assessment — 1700
rows: Patient sitting, less agitated, intermittently tearful; accepted water; continues to express belief that infant was switched
Minimal — patient states 'maybe something is wrong with me' when prompted by husband

questions.stem: The nurse reviews the triage assessment and nursing note for Ms. Okafor. Which findings are consistent with a psychiatric emergency in the postpartum period? Select all that apply.

choices.text: Fixed false belief that the infant was switched at the hospital
Auditory hallucinations
Absence of sleep for 72 hours
Blood pressure 138/88 mmHg
Rapid, tangential speech
SpO₂ 99% on room air
Postpartum day 5

questions.hint: Consider the hallmark features of postpartum psychosis: when does it typically present, and what are the defining psychiatric symptoms? Not all abnormal findings indicate a psychiatric emergency.
questions.feedback: Correct: A, B, C, E, G. Fixed delusions, auditory hallucinations, 72-hour sleep deprivation, disorganized/tangential speech, and presentation on postpartum day 5 (typically occurs within the first two weeks) are hallmark features of postpartum psychosis. Blood pressure and normal SpO₂ are physiologic findings that require monitoring but are not psychiatric emergency indicators in isolation.

questions.stem: The 1520 laboratory results are available. For each result, indicate whether the finding Supports a Medical Cause, Rules Out a Medical Cause, or Requires Further Investigation.

choices.text: TSH 2.1 mIU/L, Free T4 1.1 ng/dL
CMP within normal limits
Urine drug screen negative
Blood alcohol undetectable
WBC 9,200/mm³
Urinalysis — no WBC, bacteria, or nitrites

questions.hint: Think about what each lab test is designed to rule out. Thyroid labs, metabolic panel, drug screen, WBC, and urinalysis each target a specific reversible medical cause of acute behavioral change.
questions.feedback: All six findings rule out medical causes. Normal TSH/T4 excludes thyroid dysfunction. Normal CMP excludes metabolic/electrolyte causes. Negative drug screen and alcohol level exclude intoxication. Normal WBC excludes infectious or septic encephalopathy. Normal urinalysis excludes UTI. Collectively, these support a primary psychiatric diagnosis.

questions.stem: The nurse is preparing to implement the provider's orders. Which action should the nurse perform first?

choices.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg IM
Place the infant with the husband and initiate continuous 1:1 nursing observation
Contact the social work department to initiate a consult
Contact the lactation consultant regarding breastfeeding safety

questions.hint: In a setting of acute psychosis with an infant present, what is the priority before any medication is given? Think about what the patient cannot currently provide for herself and the infant.
questions.feedback: Correct. Ensuring infant safety and establishing continuous patient monitoring are the highest priority when a patient has impaired judgment and delusions involving the infant. Safety must be secured before pharmacologic interventions are administered.

questions.stem: The nurse is reviewing the order for haloperidol 5 mg IM in preparation for administration. Which assessment finding, if present, would require the nurse to hold the medication and notify the provider before administering?

choices.text: Heart rate 104 bpm
Blood pressure 136/86 mmHg
Patient pacing and requesting to leave the room
QTc interval of 510 ms on a newly obtained ECG

questions.hint: Review the known cardiac risks associated with this class of medication. There is a specific ECG threshold above which this medication is contraindicated due to the risk of a life-threatening dysrhythmia.
questions.feedback: Correct. Haloperidol carries a risk of QT prolongation and torsades de pointes. A QTc >500 ms is a threshold at which the risk of life-threatening dysrhythmia is significantly elevated and is a contraindication to administering QT-prolonging medications. The HR and BP do not contraindicate administration. Agitation is the indication, not a reason to withhold.

questions.stem: Categorize each nursing action as Appropriate or Not Appropriate for this clinical situation.

choices.text: Maintaining a calm, quiet environment with minimal stimulation
Explaining to the patient that her belief about the infant is incorrect and not based in reality
Providing short, clear, simple statements when communicating
Leaving the patient's room briefly to obtain supplies while the 1:1 observer is present
Documenting the patient's exact statements using quotation marks
Telling the patient she cannot leave until the psychiatrist clears her

questions.hint: Consider the therapeutic communication principles for a patient experiencing active psychosis. Confronting delusions directly and using authoritative, restrictive language can escalate agitation.
questions.feedback: Appropriate: Calm environment, short clear statements, leaving with 1:1 observer present, documenting exact statements in quotation marks. Not Appropriate: Directly confronting the delusion (escalates agitation — the patient lacks capacity to accept reality-based correction) and telling the patient she cannot leave (non-therapeutic, authoritative language; raises legal/ethical issues around involuntary detention not yet formally established).

questions.stem: At 1700, the nurse reassesses Ms. Okafor following haloperidol administration. Which finding best indicates the medication has had a therapeutic effect?

choices.text: Blood pressure decreased to 128/82 mmHg
Patient states the infant was not switched and she understands she was experiencing a delusion
Patient is sitting, less agitated, and accepted water
Patient denies suicidal and homicidal ideation

questions.hint: The expected first effect of this medication in acute psychosis is behavioral, not cognitive. Resolution of delusional thinking takes days to weeks — what would you expect to see within 70 minutes of a first dose?
questions.feedback: Correct. The therapeutic effect of haloperidol in acute psychosis is reduction in behavioral agitation — not immediate resolution of psychotic thought content. The patient is sitting, less agitated, and cooperative enough to accept water. Delusions resolve over days to weeks. The BP change is minimal and not attributable to haloperidol. SI/HI denial was present at baseline.
